If the supplier had delivered the interface document on time, we would have completed the handover in March.
If procurement had placed the order in January, the lead time would not have been a problem.
If I had prepared for that interview, I would not have frozen in the first two minutes.
If we had more time, we would run another test tomorrow.
Hello, I'm Elena Vidal, senior engineer at Thales. I've read the brief, but I would rather hear it from you. What exactly do you own on this project?
I'm responsible for the signaling workstream on the Line 5 extension. I specialize in CBTC, I coordinate the design and field teams, and I liaise with every supplier on the package.
Good. And where is the project right now, honestly?
We're currently in the process of closing the design review. If the interface document had arrived in January, we would have completed the review three months ago.
I heard about the delay. Is it only the document?

Honestly, the document is the tip of the iceberg. The lead time for the interlocking is nine months, and procurement placed the order in April. One of the main challenges we face is the handover date.
Then let us not repeat that. I will look into the interlocking with my team, and I will come back to you on Friday with a date.
I'm responsible for the signaling workstream on the Line 5 extension.
My role involves coordinating the design and field teams.
We're currently in the process of closing the design review.
If the interface document had arrived earlier, we would have completed the handover on time.
One of the main challenges we face is the lead time for the interlocking.

Hello João, Elena Vidal here, as promised. I looked into the interlocking with our procurement team this morning. The lead time is confirmed at nine months, so the equipment lands in São Paulo in January, not in October. That is the bad news. The good news is that our subcontractor can start the cabling as soon as the interface document is signed, which means the field work does not have to wait for the hardware. One of the main challenges I see is the handover date, because it still assumes an October delivery. Could you send me the updated schedule for the handover before Wednesday? I would rather bring a realistic date to the steering committee than a date nobody believes. Thank you, and talk to you on Wednesday.

Good morning, everyone. This is a lessons-learned meeting, not a court, so I want to be very clear from the start. I am not interested in blaming the subcontractor. The problem was our process, not one company. Let us look at the facts. If procurement had been involved in the design review, the order would have been placed in January and the lead time would not have hurt the schedule. If the interface document had been signed before the order, we would have avoided four months of rework. If somebody had looked into the first alarm in March, that alarm would not have become a serious problem in June. And honestly, if we had been open about the delay earlier, the client would have accepted a new date without a fight. So here is the one rule I want for the next package: the interface document is signed before any order is placed. No exceptions. Everything else on my list is negotiable, but that one is not.
